title: Networking Fundamentals and Protocols
obj: Students will be able to describe how data travels across a network, identify the layers of the OSI model, and explain the role of common networking protocols.
content: LAN, WAN, MAN, PAN; nodes, links, and switches; why networks exist
7 layers (Physical → Data Link → Network → Transport → Session → Presentation → Application); each layer's job; how layers encapsulate data
4-layer practical model that maps to OSI; dominant in real networks
HTTP/HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, DNS, DHCP — each protocol's purpose and port
how data is broken into packets; headers add addressing; frames add MAC addresses at layer 2
hub vs. switch vs. router vs. access point — where each operates in the OSI stack
assess: OSI labeling: given a scenario (e.g., browser request), identify which layer handles each step
Protocol matching: match 10 protocols to their port numbers and descriptions
Short answer: what is the difference between a switch and a router?

title: Installing and Securing Wireless Networks
obj: Students will be able to install and configure a wireless access point, set security parameters, and verify client connectivity.
content: signal range, interference sources, channel overlap; 2.4 GHz vs. 5 GHz coverage trade-offs
visible vs. hidden SSID; naming conventions; guest networks
WPA2-Personal vs. WPA2-Enterprise; WPA3; disabling WEP/WPS
router-assigned vs. static IPs for wireless clients; reservation by MAC address
non-overlapping channels (1/6/11 for 2.4 GHz); auto vs. manual selection; avoiding neighbor interference
checking signal strength, running a speed test, verifying security protocol in client adapter settings
assess: Lab: configure a consumer router with a new SSID, WPA3 password, and guest network isolation
Signal mapping: use a Wi-Fi analyzer app to map signal strength across a room
Short answer: why is WEP considered insecure and what should replace it?

title: TCP/UDP Ports and Protocols
obj: Students will be able to distinguish between TCP and UDP, identify well-known port numbers, and explain why port numbers matter for network troubleshooting.
content: TCP: reliable, ordered, connection-oriented (3-way handshake); UDP: fast, connectionless, no guarantee
port numbers 0–65535; well-known (0–1023), registered (1024–49151), dynamic (49152+); a socket = IP + port
20/21 FTP, 22 SSH, 23 Telnet, 25 SMTP, 80 HTTP, 110 POP3, 143 IMAP, 443 HTTPS, 3389 RDP
Why protocols use TCP vs UDP
real-time streaming → UDP; file transfer/email → TCP; DNS uses both
how firewalls allow/block by port; port scanning with nmap; open vs. closed vs. filtered
assess: Port quiz: identify the protocol for 15 port numbers without notes
Scenario: a user can browse websites (port 80) but cannot send email — which port is likely blocked?
Wireshark/capture lab: capture a TCP handshake and identify the SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK packets

title: Wireless Networking Technologies
obj: Students will be able to compare 802.11 Wi-Fi standards by speed, frequency, and range, and identify Bluetooth and cellular wireless technologies.
content: 802.11a/b/g/n/ac/ax (Wi-Fi 6)/be (Wi-Fi 7); frequency bands, max theoretical speeds, range
OFDMA, MU-MIMO, BSS coloring; 6 GHz band for Wi-Fi 6E; reduced congestion
versions 4.0 through 5.3; Classic vs. BLE; range and throughput; pairing process
near-field communication; 10 cm range; used for payments, access badges, device pairing
LTE/5G as a backup WAN or primary for IoT; hotspot use cases; carrier aggregation
2.4 GHz: longer range, more interference; 5/6 GHz: faster, shorter range; choosing the right band for the scenario
assess: Standards chart: complete a table comparing 802.11n, ac, and ax on frequency, speed, and range
Short answer: why would a technician enable 5 GHz only on an access point in a dense apartment building?
Hands-on: connect a device to both 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz and compare speed-test results

title: Services Provided by Networked Hosts
obj: Students will be able to describe the services that servers provide on a network, including file sharing, authentication, web, email, and print services.
content: SMB/CIFS for Windows shares, NFS for Unix/Linux; mapping drives; permissions on shared folders
HTTP/HTTPS servers (IIS, Apache, nginx); how a browser request is served; SSL certificates
SMTP for sending; IMAP and POP3 for retrieval; spam filtering; MX records
LDAP, Active Directory, RADIUS; how a domain login works; single sign-on concept
print server spooling jobs; network printers vs. local shared printers; IPP protocol
SSH, RDP, VNC; Telnet (legacy/insecure); how remote access is authenticated and encrypted
assess: Service-port matching: given 10 network services, identify the protocol and port number for each
Scenario: a user can log into their laptop but not access a shared drive — list three possible causes
Short answer: what is the difference between IMAP and POP3 for email retrieval?

title: Network Configuration Concepts (IP, DNS, DHCP)
obj: Students will be able to configure network adapter settings including static and dynamic IP, DNS servers, and default gateway, and verify connectivity using command-line tools.
content: four octets; network vs. host portion; subnet mask in decimal and CIDR notation
when to assign static IPs (servers, printers, routers) vs. let DHCP assign; lease time
router's IP on the local subnet; how traffic leaves the LAN; what happens without a gateway
primary and secondary DNS; public DNS servers (8.8.8.8, 1.1.1.1); what happens when DNS is wrong
128-bit addresses; link-local, global unicast, loopback; dual-stack environments
ipconfig /all, ping, tracert/traceroute, nslookup — reading the output
assess: Lab: configure a static IP, subnet mask, gateway, and DNS on a Windows VM and verify internet access
Output reading: given ipconfig output, identify the IP, subnet mask, gateway, and lease expiry
Short answer: a computer has an IP of 169.254.x.x — what does this indicate and how do you fix it?

title: Networking Tools and Diagnostics
obj: Students will be able to use command-line and physical networking tools to gather information, test connectivity, and diagnose network problems.
content: ICMP echo request/reply; testing reachability; TTL values; interpreting packet loss
mapping the hop-by-hop path; identifying where packets are dropped or delayed
reading and releasing/renewing IP configuration on Windows and Linux
querying DNS; checking A, MX, CNAME records; diagnosing DNS failures
active connections, listening ports, routing table; spotting unexpected connections
cable tester, toner probe/fox-and-hound, loopback plugs, Wi-Fi analyzer apps
assess: CLI lab: run ping, tracert, and nslookup on a live network and document the results
Diagnose scenario: given tracert output that shows timeout at hop 3, identify where the failure is
Short answer: what is the difference between ping and tracert?

title: Network Troubleshooting
obj: Students will be able to apply a systematic troubleshooting approach to diagnose and resolve common network connectivity problems.
content: 'no internet access' vs. 'limited connectivity' vs. 'unidentified network'; yellow triangle meanings
Physical (cable/link light) → IP config → gateway ping → DNS test → remote host ping
bad cable, wrong DHCP, IP conflict, DNS misconfiguration, blocked port, failed NIC
how APIPA addresses appear (169.254.x.x); releasing and renewing; finding the duplicate
SSID not found, wrong password, driver issues, channel congestion, weak signal
when the problem is upstream (ISP, firewall, managed switch) and should be escalated
assess: Scenario matrix: given 5 different connectivity symptoms, diagnose each using the layer-by-layer approach
Lab: deliberately misconfigure a VM's network settings and then troubleshoot back to connectivity
Documentation task: write a ticket closure note for a resolved IP conflict

title: IP Addressing and Subnetting
obj: Students will be able to classify IPv4 addresses by class, determine network and host IDs from a subnet mask, and calculate the usable host range of a subnet.
content: /8, /16, /24 CIDR; converting between CIDR and dotted-decimal; AND operation to find network ID
how many subnets / hosts: 2^n subnets, 2^h − 2 hosts; working through /26 and /28 examples
network address (all host bits = 0), broadcast (all host bits = 1); not assignable to hosts
variable-length subnet masking; fitting subnets of different sizes into an address space
128-bit hex notation; ::1 loopback; /64 prefix standard; why IPv6 was needed
assess: Subnetting worksheet: given five /24 networks to divide, calculate network address, broadcast, and host range for each
Binary subnet: convert a /27 mask to binary and perform the AND operation with an IP
Short answer: why are private IP ranges not routable on the public internet?

title: DNS and DHCP Services
obj: Students will be able to explain how DNS resolves domain names to IP addresses and how DHCP automatically assigns network configuration to clients.
content: recursive resolver → root → TLD → authoritative server; caching and TTL; browser cache
A (IPv4), AAAA (IPv6), CNAME (alias), MX (mail), PTR (reverse lookup), TXT (SPF/DKIM)
DORA: Discover → Offer → Request → Acknowledge; lease time; scope and exclusions
binding an IP to a MAC address; when to use static assignment vs. reservation
nslookup queries, flushing DNS cache (ipconfig /flushdns), checking /etc/hosts
internal vs. external DNS zones; why a hostname might resolve differently inside vs. outside the network
assess: DNS query trace: use nslookup to trace a domain resolution and describe each server involved
DHCP scenario: a new printer must always get the same IP — explain two ways to achieve this
Short answer: what happens if DHCP is unavailable when a Windows PC boots?
